ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese grilled pork breast. FOODON_03311349 (pork food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (pig meat, fresh or chilled) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code for Vietnamese-style grilled pork breast exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared pork dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Vietnamese grilled pork breast is approximately 200 g, consisting of sliced or whole grilled pork breast pieces served with rice or vermicelli.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, loin, blade (chops), bone-in, separable lean and fat, cooked, broiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Vietnamese-style grilled pork breast (vú heo nướng) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork loin/blade data (broiled), which is the closest approved analogue for marinated grilled pork breast. Marinade ingredients (lemongrass, fish sauce, garlic, sugar) contribute minor macro adjustments; sodium is elevated slightly to reflect fish sauce marinade. Values represent a reasonable estimate for the cooked dish per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure meat dishes with negligible carbohydrate content have a GI that is effectively zero or unmeasurable, as they do not raise blood glucose in a meaningful way. Following Veritas v2 scoring model convention for effectively zero-GI foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value); gi_category is low; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 given the negligible carbohydrate content per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fish sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of vú heo nướng due to elevated sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) contributed by fish sauce in the marinade; a 200 g serving provides approximately 960 mg sodium, representing 40–48% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2400 mg.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Vú heo nướng is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a protein-rich, low-carbohydrate dish (~2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with a negligible glycemic index; it supports blood glucose management when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of vú heo nướng due to its saturated fat content (~5.2 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from fish sauce marinade; grilling reduces fat compared to frying, but portion control and pairing with vegetables is advised to limit saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vú heo nướng (Vietnamese grilled pork breast) is a popular Vietnamese dish consisting of pork breast marinated in a fragrant blend of lemongrass, fish sauce, garlic, sugar, and black pepper, then grilled or chargrilled over high heat until caramelised and slightly charred on the exterior while remaining juicy inside. The dish is widely served in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore, including at establishments such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly sweet, and smoky with aromatic lemongrass notes. Texturally, the pork breast is tender with a lightly crisp, caramelised outer layer. Nutritionally, vú heo nướng is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with approximately 25.8 g protein and 14.5 g fat per 100 g, and very low carbohydrate content (~2.5 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce in the marinade (~480 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is typically served with steamed jasmine rice, vermicelli noodles, or fresh herbs and pickled vegetables. In Vietnamese cuisine, nướng (grilled) preparations are considered a healthier cooking method compared to deep-frying, as excess fat drips away during grilling.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for vú heo nướng (Vietnamese grilled pork breast) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork loin/blade (broiled) data, which is the closest approved analogue. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect fish sauce marinade contribution. Carbohydrate is adjusted slightly upward to reflect sugar in marinade. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork (broiled) data adapted for Vietnamese-style grilled pork breast with fish sauce and lemongrass marinade. Sodium_mg is elevated above baseline pork values to reflect fish sauce marinade contribution. Carbohydrate_g reflects minor sugar content from marinade. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used for this adaptation. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) following Veritas v2 scoring model convention for high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate meat dishes that do not produce a measurable glycemic response; gi_category is low; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. The food is served at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore (Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant, Circular Road). Price observed at SGD 20.00 per serving on field survey data. The cuisine is classified as Vietnamese (served in Singapore context). Halal status is not confirmed as pork is not halal.
